sections.6.1: Yes. The ATO Deduction Assistant uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground its responses in official, public tax policy documents. This data is highly suitable because it represents the authoritative source of truth for tax deduction eligibility, ensuring the chatbot's guidance remains aligned with official ATO policy. To ensure suitability and reliability, the ATO will apply the National AI Centre's Data Quality Checklist (Data Quality Checklist, p.1). Specifically, the ATO will verify the accuracy of the policy documents against trusted internal sources (Accuracy) (Data Quality Checklist, p.1), ensure completeness of the document repository so that no critical deduction categories are missing (Completeness) (Data Quality Checklist, p.1), and maintain consistency across different tax policy sources (Consistency) (Data Quality Checklist, p.1). The ATO will also ensure timeliness by keeping the policy documents up to date for the current financial year (Timeliness) (Data Quality Checklist, p.2). Furthermore, the RAG architecture maintains strict traceability (lineage), allowing the system to prove which specific tax policy document influenced a given output and explain this to users (Traceability (lineage)) (Data Quality Checklist, p.3). Finally, the ATO will verify that the data coverage is sufficient to handle all expected taxpayer scenarios and groups, minimizing bias in the guidance provided (Data coverage/bias) (Data Quality Checklist, p.3).
sections.6.2: Not applicable. The ATO Deduction Assistant is designed to process public tax policy documents and user-provided financial scenarios to provide general tax deduction guidance. It does not target, collect, or use Indigenous data, nor do its outputs specifically relate to Indigenous people. However, if any future expansion of the system incorporates Indigenous data or impacts Indigenous communities, the ATO will ensure consistency with the Framework for the Governance of Indigenous Data to support Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander agency over how their data is governed within the APS (DDGS, p.13) and protect Indigenous cultural and intellectual property (AI Use Policy v2.0, p.20).
sections.8.3: Yes. The ATO will establish processes to maintain comprehensive documentation and records throughout the lifecycle of the ATO Deduction Assistant, in accordance with National Archives of Australia (NAA) guidance. All AI-generated content created or received constitutes Commonwealth records under the Archives Act 1983 and must be managed as such (NAA AI Records Guidance, §General principles for managing records generated by AI). Since AI systems generally lack adequate records management functionality, the ATO will capture and retain these records in an approved records management system (NAA AI Records Guidance, §General principles for managing records generated by AI). The ATO will take a risk-based approach to determine which AI outputs, prompts, inputs, and sources must be retained to support transparency and accountability (NAA AI Records Guidance, §General principles for managing records generated by AI; NAA AI Records Guidance, §Disposal of generative AI assistant prompts and inputs). This includes retaining the prompts and inputs where necessary to provide credible evidence of how outputs were generated (NAA AI Records Guidance, §Disposal of generative AI assistant prompts and inputs). Furthermore, the ATO will document and retain records of system design, development, testing, and operational specifications (NAA AI Records Guidance, §Managing AI technologies incorporated into business systems). Although the ATO has not yet confirmed whether a Business System Assessment Framework (BSAF) assessment has been scheduled or conducted, the ATO acknowledges that undertaking a BSAF process is a recommended first step to identify and manage AI-generated records and ensure adequate records management functionality is in place (NAA AI Records Guidance, §Managing AI technologies incorporated into business systems). Finally, the ATO will ensure that the management of these records is addressed in its information governance framework and that relevant staff receive adequate training on their records management responsibilities (NAA AI Records Guidance, §General principles for managing records generated by AI).
